claims.claim: Each additional 300 mg of dietary cholesterol consumed per day is significantly associated with higher risk of incident CVD and all-cause mortality among US adults.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Risk of incident CVD for each additional 300 mg of dietary cholesterol.
Absolute risk difference for incident CVD.

claims.importance_rationale: This provides a direct association between dietary cholesterol intake and primary cardiovascular outcomes.
claims.relevance_rationale: This provides a direct association between dietary cholesterol intake and primary cardiovascular outcomes.
claims.source_quote: Each additional 300 mg of dietary cholesterol consumed per day was significantly associated with higher risk of incident CVD (adjusted HR, 1.17 [95% CI, 1.09-1.26]; adjusted ARD, 3.24% [95% CI, 1.39%-5.08%]) and all-cause mortality (adjusted HR, 1.18 [95% CI, 1.10-1.26]; adjusted ARD, 4.43% [95% CI, 2.51%-6.36%]).

claim_context.evidence_design: Pooled cohort study (6 prospective US cohorts)

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Risk of incident CVD for each additional 300 mg of dietary cholesterol.
Absolute risk difference for incident CVD.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Higher daily dietary cholesterol is associated with increased risk of CVD and death.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: Among US adults, higher consumption of dietary cholesterol or eggs is significantly associated with a higher risk of incident cardiovascular disease (CVD) and all-cause mortality in a dose-response manner, independent of fat quality and overall diet quality.

supporting_quotes.quote: Each additional 300 mg of dietary cholesterol consumed per day was significantly associated with higher risk of incident CVD (adjusted HR, 1.17 [95% CI, 1.09-1.26]; adjusted ARD, 3.24% [95% CI, 1.39%-5.08%]) and all-cause mortality (adjusted HR, 1.18 [95% CI, 1.10-1.26]; adjusted ARD, 4.43% [95% CI, 2.51%-6.36%]).

claims.claim: Each additional half an egg consumed per day is significantly associated with higher risk of incident CVD and all-cause mortality.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Risk of incident CVD for each additional half egg.
Absolute risk difference for incident CVD.

claims.importance_rationale: This addresses the specific impact of egg consumption as a primary source of dietary cholesterol.
claims.relevance_rationale: This addresses the specific impact of egg consumption as a primary source of dietary cholesterol.
claims.source_quote: Each additional half an egg consumed per day was significantly associated with higher risk of incident CVD (adjusted HR, 1.06 [95% CI, 1.03-1.10]; adjusted ARD, 1.11% [95% CI, 0.32%-1.89%]) and all-cause mortality (adjusted HR, 1.08 [95% CI, 1.04-1.11]; adjusted ARD, 1.93% [95% CI, 1.10%-2.76%]).

claim_context.evidence_design: Pooled cohort study (6 prospective US cohorts)

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Risk of incident CVD for each additional half egg.
Absolute risk difference for incident CVD.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Higher daily egg consumption is associated with increased CVD risk and mortality.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: Among US adults, higher consumption of dietary cholesterol or eggs is significantly associated with a higher risk of incident cardiovascular disease (CVD) and all-cause mortality in a dose-response manner, independent of fat quality and overall diet quality.

supporting_quotes.quote: Each additional half an egg consumed per day was significantly associated with higher risk of incident CVD (adjusted HR, 1.06 [95% CI, 1.03-1.10]; adjusted ARD, 1.11% [95% CI, 0.32%-1.89%]) and all-cause mortality (adjusted HR, 1.08 [95% CI, 1.04-1.11]; adjusted ARD, 1.93% [95% CI, 1.10%-2.76%]).

claims.claim: The association between egg consumption and incident CVD was no longer significant after adjusting for dietary cholesterol consumption.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Non-significant association for CVD after adjusting for cholesterol.

claims.importance_rationale: This indicates that the risk associated with eggs is primarily driven by their cholesterol content rather than other unique components of the egg.
claims.relevance_rationale: This indicates that the risk associated with eggs is primarily driven by their cholesterol content rather than other unique components of the egg.
claims.source_quote: The associations between egg consumption and incident CVD (adjusted HR, 0.99 [95% CI, 0.93-1.05]; adjusted ARD, −0.47% [95% CI, −1.83% to 0.88%]) and all-cause mortality (adjusted HR, 1.03 [95% CI, 0.97-1.09]; adjusted ARD, 0.71% [95% CI, −0.85% to 2.28%]) were no longer significant after adjusting for dietary cholesterol consumption.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Non-significant association for CVD after adjusting for cholesterol.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: The CVD risk from eggs is fully accounted for by the cholesterol they contain.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: Among US adults, higher consumption of dietary cholesterol or eggs is significantly associated with a higher risk of incident cardiovascular disease (CVD) and all-cause mortality in a dose-response manner, independent of fat quality and overall diet quality.

supporting_quotes.quote: The associations between egg consumption and incident CVD (adjusted HR, 0.99 [95% CI, 0.93-1.05]; adjusted ARD, −0.47% [95% CI, −1.83% to 0.88%]) and all-cause mortality (adjusted HR, 1.03 [95% CI, 0.97-1.09]; adjusted ARD, 0.71% [95% CI, −0.85% to 2.28%]) were no longer significant after adjusting for dietary cholesterol consumption.

claims.claim: The association between dietary cholesterol consumption and incident CVD was stronger in participants with a lower BMI (<25) than in overweight or obese participants.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Risk of CVD for BMI <25.
Statistical significance of the difference between BMI subgroups.

claims.importance_rationale: Identifies a specific population (lower BMI) where the association with cholesterol is more pronounced.
claims.relevance_rationale: Identifies a specific population (lower BMI) where the association with cholesterol is more pronounced.
claims.source_quote: The association between dietary cholesterol consumption (per 300 mg/day) and incident CVD was stronger in participants with BMI lower than 25 (adjusted HR, 1.25 [95% CI, 1.12-1.39]) than in overweight participants (BMI ≥25 to <30; adjusted HR, 1.05 [95% CI, 0.95-1.16]) or obese participants (BMI ≥30; adjusted HR, 1.14 [95% CI, 1.03-1.26]) (P value for interaction, .03)

claim_context.evidence_design: Subgroup analysis of pooled cohort study

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Risk of CVD for BMI <25.
Statistical significance of the difference between BMI subgroups.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: The link between dietary cholesterol and CVD is stronger in individuals with a lower BMI.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: Among US adults, higher consumption of dietary cholesterol or eggs is significantly associated with a higher risk of incident cardiovascular disease (CVD) and all-cause mortality in a dose-response manner, independent of fat quality and overall diet quality.

supporting_quotes.quote: The association between dietary cholesterol consumption (per 300 mg/day) and incident CVD was stronger in participants with BMI lower than 25 (adjusted HR, 1.25 [95% CI, 1.12-1.39]) than in overweight participants (BMI ≥25 to <30; adjusted HR, 1.05 [95% CI, 0.95-1.16]) or obese participants (BMI ≥30; adjusted HR, 1.14 [95% CI, 1.03-1.26]) (P value for interaction, .03)

claims.claim: The association between dietary cholesterol consumption and all-cause mortality was stronger in women than in men.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Risk of mortality for women.
Statistical significance of the difference between sexes.

claims.importance_rationale: Identifies a sex-based difference in the association between cholesterol and mortality.
claims.relevance_rationale: Identifies a sex-based difference in the association between cholesterol and mortality.
claims.source_quote: The association between dietary cholesterol consumption and all-cause mortality was stronger in women (adjusted HR, 1.28 [95% CI, 1.17-1.41]) than in men (adjusted HR, 1.14 [95% CI, 1.06-1.22]) (P value for interaction, .02)

claim_context.evidence_design: Subgroup analysis of pooled cohort study

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Risk of mortality for women.
Statistical significance of the difference between sexes.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: The link between dietary cholesterol and all-cause mortality is stronger in women than in men.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: Among US adults, higher consumption of dietary cholesterol or eggs is significantly associated with a higher risk of incident cardiovascular disease (CVD) and all-cause mortality in a dose-response manner, independent of fat quality and overall diet quality.

supporting_quotes.quote: The association between dietary cholesterol consumption and all-cause mortality was stronger in women (adjusted HR, 1.28 [95% CI, 1.17-1.41]) than in men (adjusted HR, 1.14 [95% CI, 1.06-1.22]) (P value for interaction, .02)

claims.claim: The association between dietary cholesterol consumption and all-cause mortality was independent of fat quality (saturated vs. unsaturated) and overall diet quality.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Risk of mortality for high-saturated fat diet.
Risk of mortality for low-saturated fat diet.

claims.importance_rationale: Confirms that the association is not merely a byproduct of high saturated fat intake or poor overall eating habits.
claims.relevance_rationale: Confirms that the association is not merely a byproduct of high saturated fat intake or poor overall eating habits.
claims.source_quote: The current study found that the significant associations of dietary cholesterol consumption with incident CVD and all-cause mortality were independent of fat amount and quality of the diet.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Risk of mortality for high-saturated fat diet.
Risk of mortality for low-saturated fat diet.

must_preserve_terms: independent of fat amount and quality
whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: The link between dietary cholesterol and mortality remains even when accounting for fat quality and diet quality.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: Among US adults, higher consumption of dietary cholesterol or eggs is significantly associated with a higher risk of incident cardiovascular disease (CVD) and all-cause mortality in a dose-response manner, independent of fat quality and overall diet quality.

supporting_quotes.quote: The current study found that the significant associations of dietary cholesterol consumption with incident CVD and all-cause mortality were independent of fat amount and quality of the diet.

the significant associations of dietary cholesterol or egg consumption with incident CVD and all-cause mortality were similar between participants who consumed a higher-quality diet and those who did not
